summary: DHH responds to a debate about Google's relationship with open source, arguing that the real purpose of open source is to empower small businesses, not to serve big tech companies. He pushes back on the claim that 'open source doesn't work' just because large corporations extract more value than they contribute. DHH frames open source as a bootstrapping mechanism that lets small teams compete with companies that have armies of programmers. He uses Basecamp and Rails as examples of how open source enables small businesses to build and share tools that improve through community contribution.
key_points: Open source exists primarily for and by small businesses, not for big tech companies
Big companies extracting disproportionate value from open source doesn't make it a zero-sum game
Open source is a coordination mechanism that lets small teams compete with large corporate engineering armies
The success metric for open source should be whether it helps small businesses achieve more, not whether it satisfies big company demands
money_quotes: Open source is the route that allows us to escape the hierarchies of old and form markets of coordination for mutually beneficial progress.
It's bootstrapping a competitive alternative to having an army of 50,000 programmers kept on a campus.
Even when the big guys are extracting a lot more value than they're adding, it's not a zero sum game.
The primary line is that open source helps small businesses and individuals achieve bigger and better things that they could on their own or from buying proprietary systems.
Most open source software is simply not done for them. It's done for small businesses. By small businesses.
key_insight: Open source is fundamentally a small-business empowerment tool that lets independent developers compete with corporate giants, and its value shouldn't be judged by whether it satisfies big tech's demands.
